name: Lubaba bint al-Harith
epithet: The First Woman to Believe After Khadijah
title: Lubaba bint al-Harith (RA): The First Woman to Believe After Khadijah
meta_description: The story of Lubaba bint al-Harith (RA), known as Umm al-Fadl: the first woman to accept Islam after Khadijah, mother of Abdullah ibn Abbas, and the great aunt of the early Muslim community.
keywords: Lubaba bint al-Harith, Umm al-Fadl, first woman to believe after Khadijah, mother of Abdullah ibn Abbas, sahaba stories, women of Islam
lede: She is in almost everyone's story, and almost no one tells hers. Pull the name of nearly any early Muslim from the records and somewhere in the family tree you find her: a sister-in-law of the Prophet ﷺ twice over, an aunt of Khalid ibn al-Walid, the mother of the great scholar Abdullah ibn Abbas.

This is the story of Lubaba bint al-Harith (may Allah be pleased with her), known to the community as Umm al-Fadl: the first woman to accept Islam after Khadijah, and a quiet pillar in the lives of the people we remember most.

paras: Lubaba came from a tribe called Banu Hilal, and she was sometimes called Lubaba al-Hilaliyya. It was a Bedouin tribe known for surviving the hardest conditions of the desert. They were skilled at growing crops where little would grow, and trusted to care for people's cattle, sheep, and camels.

Families would send their children to live among Banu Hilal so they could learn to handle rough circumstances. To have been raised among them was something you could be proud of. Some say this is where her nephew Khalid ibn al-Walid first learned the toughness that later defined him.

Lubaba carried the marks of that upbringing. She was strong and courageous, a woman with a presence that could unsettle even a man like Abu Lahab. She would marry al-Abbas, the uncle of the Prophet ﷺ, and become, in time, the great aunt of the whole Muslim community.

Above every honour carried in her family ties, Lubaba held one distinction she treasured most: she was the first woman to accept Islam after Khadijah (may Allah be pleased with her). She even said she embraced Islam on the same day that Khadijah did.

She did not need to be argued into it. She knew the character of the Prophet ﷺ, she loved him, she admired him. As soon as she heard what had happened to him, she went straight to him and accepted his message. The scholar al-Dhahabi records that no woman is said to have believed before her except Khadijah, and Ibn Hajar notes the only possible exception is Fatima bint al-Khattab, the sister of Umar.

She had been close to Khadijah, and always spoke of her warmly. Through that bond, and through her marriage to al-Abbas, Lubaba became one of the few who could sit and learn from the Prophet ﷺ both in his own house and in the house of al-Arqam. In Makkah, that was a rare privilege.

sections.h2: Believing while the household held back
paras: Her son Abdullah ibn Abbas later remembered his mother as someone who declared her faith openly from the start. He said that he and his mother were counted among the weak and vulnerable believers of Makkah, the ones who suffered for what they believed.

Her husband was a different matter. Al-Abbas wanted to follow the path of Abu Talib: the loyal uncle who protected the Prophet ﷺ out of family and tribal duty, while staying back from the faith itself. He would not openly accept Islam for nearly two decades after his wife.

So Lubaba prayed. She made dua that al-Abbas would come to Islam, and she made dua that he would give up riba, the lending practice he was known for. Her nature recoiled from it long before any verse forbade it. She kept urging him, gently and persistently, toward the religion of his nephew.

sections.h2: Born in the years of the boycott
paras: When Quraysh boycotted the Prophet's clan, the whole family was made to suffer, whether they believed or not. They were cut off, left to go hungry, pressured to abandon the message so the cruelty would end. It was in those bitter days that Khadijah died, and that Abu Talib's strength gave way.

In the middle of all of it, Lubaba was carrying a child. During the boycott she gave birth to Abdullah ibn Abbas, the boy who would grow into the great scholar of this ummah. A blessing arriving in the hardest season is felt all the more, and the Prophet ﷺ loved him deeply.

When the baby was born, Lubaba asked for him to be brought to the Prophet ﷺ for the tahnik, the gentle rubbing of a date on the roof of the mouth. But there were no dates to be found in the boycott. So the Prophet ﷺ placed only his own saliva in the child's mouth. Mujahid said Abdullah was the only child with that distinction.

sections.h2: The woman who was not afraid
paras: Years later came the Battle of Badr. Al-Abbas was forced out with the Makkans, though he had no wish to fight, and the Prophet ﷺ had told his companions to spare him and capture him rather than kill him. Word of the defeat reached Makkah before the captives did.

Abu Rafi, the freed slave of al-Abbas and a vulnerable believer, heard the news that huge unseen warriors had fought beside the Muslims and could not be touched. He cried out in joy that these were the angels. Abu Lahab, enraged, threw him to the ground and beat him until he bled.

Then Lubaba acted. She seized a tent pole and struck Abu Lahab across the head, splitting it open, and stood over him. "Do you think you can overpower him just because his master is away?" The blow was so severe that he could not rise. Within days, Abu Lahab was dead. The man the Quran names with his punishment met his end at the hand of this woman.

When al-Abbas was brought as a captive, the Prophet ﷺ would show him no favour. He told him to ransom himself like everyone else. Al-Abbas protested that he had no such money. The Prophet ﷺ then asked about the wealth he had secretly left with Umm al-Fadl, telling her to keep it for their sons if he did not return.

No one had known of that conversation but the two of them. Al-Abbas said, "I bear witness that you are the Messenger of Allah, for no one knew of this except myself and Umm al-Fadl." He ransomed himself with the wealth he had hidden away with her.
It was about these captives that the verse came down, telling the Prophet ﷺ what to say to the men held in his hands:

ayah.text: Prophet, tell those you have taken captive, 'If God knows of any good in your hearts, He will give you something better than what has been taken from you, and He will forgive you: God is forgiving and merciful.'

sections.h2: Close to the Prophet to the end
paras: After the Treaty of Hudaybiyya, al-Abbas finally accepted Islam openly, and the family moved to Madinah for the last years of the Prophet's life. There her sons stayed near him: Abdullah shadowing him, fetching his water, watching his night prayer, carrying so much of what we now know. Much of what we have about the farewell Hajj comes through her son al-Fadl, who rode behind the Prophet ﷺ.

On the day of Arafah, the companions did not know whether they should be fasting. The heat was heavy and they would not interrupt the Prophet's long dua to ask. Lubaba understood. She sent him a cup of milk. When he raised it and drank, the answer was clear to everyone, and they drank too. Her quiet wisdom had eased them out of their difficulty.

She also became the wet nurse of al-Husayn, the beloved grandson of the Prophet ﷺ, after she dreamed that a part of his flesh was in her home. Once, when she lightly smacked the crying infant, the Prophet ﷺ told her, "You have hurt me by hurting my son." Lubaba lived until the time of Uthman, and was buried in al-Baqi.

lessons_intro: Her name sits in the background of a hundred famous stories. What she shows us is how much good a steady, faithful person can carry without ever standing in the spotlight.

lessons.bold: Believe the moment you know the truth.
lessons.text: Lubaba did not wait to be persuaded or to see which way her household would lean. She knew his character, and she went straight to him.

lessons.bold: Keep praying for the people you love.
lessons.text: For nearly twenty years she made dua for her husband to find faith, and kept gently urging him. Patience with those close to us is its own kind of strength.

lessons.bold: Courage is standing up when it is needed.
lessons.text: She defended a beaten servant against a powerful man without a moment's hesitation. Quiet people can be the bravest in the room.

lessons.bold: Small acts of wisdom serve everyone.
lessons.text: A single cup of milk, offered at the right moment, settled a question for a whole crowd. Attentiveness to others is a quiet form of service.

faq.a: She was the wife of al-Abbas, the uncle of the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ, and the mother of Abdullah ibn Abbas. Known as Umm al-Fadl, she is remembered as the first woman to accept Islam after Khadijah (RA).

faq.q: Why is she called the first woman to believe after Khadijah?
faq.a: As soon as she heard about the Prophet's message she went to him and accepted Islam, saying she did so on the same day as Khadijah. Scholars record that no woman is known to have believed before her except Khadijah, with the possible exception of Fatima bint al-Khattab.

faq.q: What is the story of Lubaba and Abu Lahab?
faq.a: When Abu Lahab beat the believing servant Abu Rafi for celebrating the victory at Badr, Lubaba struck Abu Lahab on the head with a tent pole and stood over him. He never recovered and died within days.

faq.q: What can we learn from the life of Lubaba?
faq.a: To accept the truth without delay, to keep praying patiently for those we love, to find courage when it is needed, and to serve others through quiet, attentive wisdom.

closing_heading: Why this story stays with us
closing: Lubaba is easy to overlook. She is the relative in the margin of the famous accounts, the one who hands over a cup of milk, the one who prays for years before anyone sees the answer. And yet so much of the good we inherit passed through her hands and her home.

Most of us will live that way too: not in the headlines, but in the steady work of believing early, praying for our families, standing up when someone is wronged, and noticing what the people around us quietly need. Her life is an invitation to take that work seriously. Carry one piece of it into your day, and her story has done its work.
source_note: The narrative follows the account of Lubaba bint al-Harith (RA) in Dr. Omar Suleiman's series The Firsts (Yaqeen Institute). The Quran translation is from The Qur'an, M.A.S. Abdel Haleem (Quran 8:70). Where the historical record carries more than one narration, we have followed the most widely reported.

slides:
She is in almost everyone's story.

And almost no one tells hers.

This is Lubaba bint al-Harith (RA), known as Umm al-Fadl.

She came from Banu Hilal, a desert tribe known for surviving the harshest conditions.

She was strong, courageous, and unafraid: a presence that could even unsettle Abu Lahab.

Her greatest honour was not her family ties.

It was this: she was the **first woman to accept Islam after Khadijah**, on the same day, she said, that Khadijah believed.

Her husband held back from faith for nearly twenty years.

So she prayed. For his guidance, and for him to give up riba. She kept urging him, gently, toward the truth.

Her son Abdullah ibn Abbas was born during the boycott.

There were no dates for the tahnik, so the Prophet ﷺ placed only his own saliva in the baby's mouth.

When Abu Lahab beat a believer who celebrated the victory at Badr, Lubaba seized a tent pole and struck him on the head.

Within days, Abu Lahab was dead.

On the day of Arafah, no one knew whether to fast.

Lubaba sent the Prophet ﷺ a cup of milk. He drank it, and the whole crowd had their answer.

**Believe the moment you know the truth.**

She did not wait to be persuaded. She knew his character, and she went.

May Allah be pleased with her.
social.caption:
She sits in the background of a hundred famous stories, and almost no one tells her own. Lubaba bint al-Harith (may Allah be pleased with her), known as Umm al-Fadl, was the first woman to accept Islam after Khadijah.

She was the mother of the great scholar Abdullah ibn Abbas, the aunt of Khalid ibn al-Walid, and a sister-in-law of the Prophet ﷺ twice over. She prayed for years for her husband to find faith, defended a beaten believer against Abu Lahab, and once eased a whole crowd's worry with a single cup of milk.

Which part of her story stayed with you? 🤍

Save this for later, and share it with someone who would love it.

Read the full story: buruja.com/companions/lubaba-bint-al-harith

email.subject: The first woman to believe after Khadijah
email.alt_subject: The aunt who is in almost everyone's story
email.preview: She prayed for years, stood up to Abu Lahab, and is remembered by almost no one.
email.body:
Assalamu alaikum,

There is a woman whose name sits quietly inside a hundred famous stories, and almost no one tells her own. Her name is Lubaba bint al-Harith (may Allah be pleased with her), known to the early community as Umm al-Fadl.

She was the wife of al-Abbas, the uncle of the Prophet ﷺ, and the mother of Abdullah ibn Abbas, who became the great scholar of this ummah. But the honour she treasured most was simpler than any of that. She was the first woman to accept Islam after Khadijah. She said she believed on the same day that Khadijah did.

She did not need persuading. She knew the character of the Prophet ﷺ, and as soon as she heard his message, she went to him and accepted it.

What moves me about her is the patience. Her husband held back from faith for nearly twenty years. So she prayed for him, year after year, and kept gently urging him toward the truth. She did not give up on the person closest to her.

She was not timid, either. When Abu Lahab beat a believing servant for celebrating the victory at Badr, Lubaba seized a tent pole and struck him on the head, standing over him as she did. Within days, that enemy of the Prophet ﷺ was dead.

And she was wise in small, quiet ways. On the day of Arafah, when the companions did not know whether they should be fasting and would not interrupt the Prophet's dua to ask, she simply sent him a cup of milk. He drank it, and the whole crowd had their answer.

Her life is a reminder that you do not have to stand in the spotlight to carry real good. You can believe early, pray for your family, stand up when someone is wronged, and notice what the people around you quietly need.

May Allah be pleased with her, and gather us in the company of those who believed early and served faithfully.

If you would like the full story, we have written it here:

paras: She is in almost everyone's story, and almost no one tells hers. Lubaba bint al-Harith (may Allah be pleased with her), known as Umm al-Fadl, was a sister-in-law of the Prophet ﷺ twice over, an aunt of Khalid ibn al-Walid, and the mother of the great scholar Abdullah ibn Abbas.

She came from Banu Hilal, a desert tribe known for surviving the hardest conditions. She grew up strong and fearless, a woman whose presence could unsettle even a man like Abu Lahab. But the honour she held above every family tie was this: she was the first woman to accept Islam after Khadijah, on the same day, she said, that Khadijah believed.

Her husband, al-Abbas, held back from faith for nearly twenty years. So Lubaba prayed. She made dua for his guidance, and for him to give up the lending he was known for, and she kept gently urging him toward the religion of his nephew. During the years of the boycott, in the middle of all that hardship, she gave birth to Abdullah, the boy the Prophet ﷺ would love so dearly.

She was brave when it counted. When Abu Lahab threw a believing servant to the ground and beat him for celebrating the victory at Badr, Lubaba took up a tent pole and struck Abu Lahab across the head, then stood over him. "Do you think you can overpower him just because his master is away?" Within days, that enemy of the Prophet ﷺ was dead.

After the family moved to Madinah, her sons stayed close to the Prophet ﷺ, carrying so much of what we now know of his life. And on the day of Arafah, when the companions did not know whether to fast and would not interrupt his long dua, it was Lubaba who quietly sent him a cup of milk. When he drank it, everyone understood, and drank too.

She became the wet nurse of al-Husayn, the beloved grandson of the Prophet ﷺ. When she once lightly smacked the crying baby, the Prophet ﷺ told her, "You have hurt me by hurting my son." Lubaba lived until the time of Uthman, and was buried in al-Baqi, the great aunt of a community that still remembers her children better than it remembers her.
storybook.pullquote: Do you think you can overpower him just because his master is away?
storybook.reflection: Lubaba spent her life in the background: believing early, praying for her family for years, standing up when someone was wronged, easing a crowd's worry with a cup of milk. Most of us are tested the same way, away from any spotlight. Her life is an invitation to take that quiet, faithful work seriously, because it carries more than we ever see.
storybook.source: Her story follows the account in Dr. Omar Suleiman's series The Firsts.
